Follows, 	a 	c~llection 	of 	previously published essays,
articles, 	blogs, 	weekly 	faxes (sent 	to 	Diamond
Members), and other material, 	all 	related 	to 	"prospering
in un-prosperous 	times". 	At the end, 'The 	Plan,' 	invites
you 	to 	organize key ideas from these 	writings 	into
action strategies 	of 	your own.

-- 2 of 60 --

INTRODUCTION
When 	I was 	just 	getting 	my 	adult 	act 	together, 	finding 	my way 	out 	of high 	school 	and 	into
business, 	the brothers 	of 	the 	girl 	I 	was 	dating, 	a dentist's 	daughter, 	worked 	as 	car 	parking
valets 	at a 	local, 	upscale 	restaurant, 	called 	LANNINGS. 	Christmas 	Eve 	was 	a big 	night
there, 	but 	New 	Years 	Eve 	was 	THE 	big 	night. 	People 	made 	reservations 	months 	in 	advance
and 	made 	certain 	to 	arrive 	early 	because 	the 	bar 	was 	filled 	to 	SRO 	with 	vultures 	hoping 	for
no-shows 	and 	unexpectedly 	available 	tables. 	For 	the 	record, 	New Years 	Eve 	rolls 	around
here 	in northeastern 	Ohio 	in 	winter. 	Sometimes, 	by 	then, 	we 	are 	ears 	deep 	in 	snow and
bitter 	cold. 	Other 	times, 	still 	mild 	weather, 	only pregnant 	with 	gloom 	and 	miserable 	weather
to 	come. 	Other 	times, 	in 	the 	40's 	instead 	of freezing, 	but 	besieged 	with 	rain-snow-sleet, 	ice,
wind. 	The 	youngest 	of the 	two 	brothers, 	from 	whom 	I 	got 	my 	first 	business 	loan 	handed
over 	by 	him 	in 	coffee 	cans 	of coins 	from 	und~r 	bed, 	told 	me 	that - 	even though 	they 	stood
outside 	without 	cover 	in 	starched 	shirts 	and 	bow 	ties 	and 	unlined 	red jackets 	from 	5:00 	PM
until 	1:00 	AM, 	and 	had 	to 	park 	2/3rds 	of the 	cars 	in 	the 	emptY. office 	complex parking lot
across 	the 	street, r 	· 	back 	and 	forth 	all 	night, 	they 	hoped 	and 	prayed 	and 	dreamed 	of
having the 	worst, 	most 	vile, 	most 	violent 	and 	nasty 	mix 	of 	rain, 	snow, 	sleet, 	wind 	and 	cold
possible 	on 	New Years 	Eve. 	Because 	when 	that happened, 	tips quadrupled.
Of course, 	they were 	paying for 	the 	lion's 	share 	of their 	college 	educations, 	not 	expecting 	or
getting 	them 	for 	free, 	so 	they were 	motivated. 	They had 	goals .
•	"~· 	GS 	is 	still 	thriving. 	I 	went 	there a 	couple 	years 	back 	for 	New Years 	Eve. 	And 	the
principle 	enunciated 	by the 	brothers-not-grim 	is 	also 	unchanged. 	The 	worst 	of weather
makes 	opportunity 	quadruple 	for 	those 	willing 	to 	wade 	out 	into 	it 	and 	collect 	money.
I 	live 	in 	a 	place 	where 	there's 	a lot 	of weather. 	Carla 	is 	interested 	in the 	TV 	weather 	reports
so 	when 	she's here 	we 	watch 	it 	on 	the 	11:00 	PM 	news. 	If I'm 	by myself, 	I 	never 	do ·	. Never
check 	it. 	Just 	like 	I 	never 	even 	think 	about 	the 	price 	of gas. 	Because 	I 	am 	going where 	I'm
going 	and 	I'm 	going to 	do 	what 	I'm 	going 	to 	do 	regardless 	of the 	weather 	or 	the 	price 	of gas.
I 	have 	NEVER 	let 	such 	things 	dictate 	to 	me. 	NEVER. 	Not 	in 	1979, 	1989, 	1999 	and 	not 	in
2009. 	I 	might 	add: 	entire 	societies 	or 	individuals 	choose their 	dictators. 	Which 	is 	why, 	so
often, 	liberators 	are 	shocked 	to 	fmd 	themselves 	unwelcome 	- 	if they 	really wanted
liberated, 	they'd 	be 	liberated.
Somebody thinks: 	well, 	it's 	easy for 	you 	not 	to 	let 	weather 	or 	gas 	prices 	dictate; 	you 	have 	a
good, 	new 	4-wheel 	drive 	car 	with 	good 	tires 	and 	you 	are 	rich. 	No. 	I 	am 	rich 	because 	when 	I
had 	old, 	bad, 	rusty, 	leaky, 	falling-apart 	cars 	and 	bald 	tires 	and 	no 	money I 	still 	did 	not
permit 	circumstances 	to 	dictate. 	I 	am, 	incidenta.lly, 	spealdng 	both literally 	and
metaphorically, 	for 	those 	keeping 	score.
-Anyway, 	happy, 	happy, 	happy 	new year.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 60 --

m 	VAlUE 	DETERMINATE
Legitimate business/financial success (or 	personal 	career success) 	is
always 	a market assessment and 	reflection 	of 	value .
.
When Tom Monaghan 	e~tered 	an 	already 	crowded, 	in 	fact, over-populated
industry with over-met consumer needs and 	quickly 	got 	very, 	very 	rich 	with
Dominos Pizza, he 	did 	so 	because he created new and 	marketable 	value.
Personally, 	he made 	himself 	more 	valuable 	by 	study 	and research and
thought; he 	took 	time, 	for 	example, 	to 	travel 	and 	visit 	successful 	pizza
operations 	all 	over the country, to see what he 	could 	see. For 	the 	business,
he created 	value 	by 	solving 	the 	problems 	of 	delivery~ 	a means 	of 	keeping. 	.
the pizza hot in the delivery 	vehicles, 	superior 	~outing, 	etc. - 	so 	that 	he
could 	make a different 	a~d 	compelling 	pro~ise 	(in 	30 	minutes 	or 	less) 	and
back 	it 	up with a guarantee ....and 	by 	developing 	a 	lower 	entry 	cost 	·
franchis~ .... and 	by 	devising a way 	for 	owners to encourage 	good 	managers
to 	become franchisees, with 	financial 	reward 	to 	their 	'sponsor'. 	It 	would
not matter; 	it 	does 	not 	matter what the 	overall 	economic 	conditions 	are -
"good" 	or 	"bad", 	when a person makes 	himself 	more 	valuable 	personally,
and creates more and new 	value 	in a business 	for 	its 	constituencies
(customers, franchisees, investors, vendors, 	employees), 	he prospers.
'
•
. - 	. .. 	--
•
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 60 --

ore,
The mayor 	of 	a small town once wrote 	to 	Benjamin Franklin asking for a donation so the
town could buy a bell for its town square. Franklin sent money 	with 	a note 	SUCTCTestinCT 	they
forego the bell in favor 	of 	buying books for the town library. It is at the 	libr;yowe 	nright
find an answer to why so few succeed 	and 	why most fail - at anything, at everything.
Mos~ 	people do not apply themselves 	to 	acquiring know-how nor apply the know-how they
acquire. 	In short, they have the attention span 	of 	a gnat, the diligence 	of 	an 	idle, random
breeze. They certainly 	don't 	study.
I 	have become 	~uite 	rich and somewhat celebrated, reaching the pinnacle of success in not
one but three dtfferent fields. At each required skill-set, 	I 	once sucked. For me, there has
alw~ys 	been a crawl 	to 	competence, then a fast rise 	to 	superiority. 	Part 	of 	the process is
getttng through 	of 	a lot 	of infortnation in a hurry but also continuously. For nearly 	25 	years,
I 	read a book a day plus newspapers, trade journals, newsletters, visited the public library
weekly; took on a needed skill and so thoroughly and intensely studied it 	as 	to become a
world class expert. When 	I 	was teaching myself to be an advertising copywriter, for example,
I 	studied no less 	than 	an hour everyday, listened to recorded material on the subject
constantly, sought 	out 	and got to know the top people in the field, and when one told me to
take great direct-response ads and write them out longhand 	21 	times each, to teach my
subconscious the rhythm 	of 	such writing, 	I 	did that with 	100 	ads. 	I 	collected over 	200 	books
on the subject and immersed myself in them. 	I 	built organized files 	of samples that 	fill 	a
room. 	I 	traced one master back to his teachers, they to theirs, thus even knowing the
genealogy 	of the field. When 	I 	am asked by fledgling or journeymen copywriters how they,
too, might have clients waiting in line 	to 	pay them 	$100,000.00 	fees when there are
thousands 	of 	copywriters advertising their availability for 	1/lOth 	that or less, and I tell them
this answer, they reject it. 	They 	seek 	rewards 	out 	of 	kilter with 	their 	value 	and 	are
unwilling 	to 	do 	what 	is necessary 	to 	build 	up 	their 	value.
The same answer could be given by the top earners in insurance, real estate, retail store
ownership, dentistry - name the business or profession. The answer is the same.
I 	atn 	told 	by 	people 	all 	the 	time 	that 	they simply 	do 	not 	have time 	to 	read 	and 	listen 	to
all 	the 	material 	they 	have 	purchased 	or 	subscribed 	to. ·But 	time is democratic 	and 	just.
Everyone has the same amount. When 	I 	choose to read with my mid-morning coffee break
and you choose to blather about trivia with friends; when 	I 	choose to study for an 	·	hour 	sitting
on 	my 	backyard deck at day's end but 	you 	choose to watch a 	TIVO' d American Idol episode,
we reveal much. When someone says 	he 	does not have the time 	to 	apply himself to acquiring
the know-how required 	to 	create sufficient value 	f~r 	his stated desires, he is a 	~armer
surrounded by ripe fruit and vegetables, whole 	~mn~ 	~~ 	a herd 	~f c.a~e 	on. his own
property who dies 	of 	starvation, unable 	to 	orgaruze his 	time 	and 	dtsetphne 	himself to eat.
Incidentally, success 	in 	every business, including 	yo~, 	depend.s 	o?_r:wstery 	of a handful of
critical competencies (one 	of which is always 	marketing) .. 	The 	mdt~tdual 	wh.o 	sets out . 	.
earnestly and diligently to acquire a wealth 	of kno~-ho~ 	m 	each 	wmds 	~~ 	~th 	wealth 	m 	his
bank account. All others watch 	with 	envy and cry 	tn 	therr 	soup, 	two 	actiVIties 	they 	do 	seem
to 	find time 	for.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 60 --

There's 	A	Reason
I. 	t 	s had his office walls adorned top to bottom, side to side, with
One 	of my 	ear test 	men 	or 	. d 	.& 	h. 	If 	If
· 	· 	· 	· 	t 	ded 	as 	cautions to others 	as 	well as remtn ers 	10r 	tmse 	.btg 	handwntten 	stgns 	tn 	en 	h 	1 	T
' 	b 	· 	d. 	ct sales environment you've probably seen sue 	a p ace. 	woyou've ever 	een 	tn 	a 	tre 	' 	, 	"Y 	C 	H.
f 	h 	b. 	cr 	t 	s1·gns 	read 	"Thumb-Suckers 	Not Welcome Here 	and 	ou 	an 	Ireo 	t 	e 	tg0 eS
Spellers For Minimum Wage".
He 	was 	a millionaire, and in one company, held a commission record at the time 	o~
slightly over $1-million earned in 37 months. But 	h~ 	~ould 	not spell. And flaunted 	It.. 	He
said 	that, 	since 	you 	could 	hire 	perfect spellers for mtrumum wage, he preferred learning
and 	thinking about more important things, things it was not so easy to pay 	ch~aply 	for.
He 	was 	talkincr 	about the concept 	of value. 	Value 	is 	fundarnental. 	In boom ttmes or0 	• 	.
tough 	times, 	in 	big business or small business or any kind 	of business, 	even In any
occupation or career, the person who makes himself most valuable and creates the most
value 	for 	others 	(as 	they perceive it and are willing to pay for it) always prospers. 	If your
income 	isn't 	where you'd like 	it 	to be, you're 	not 	creating enough value. 	His advice
to 	me, 	said less elegantly than this, was to find and master something so valuable to
enough 	other people that they will 	cheetfully 	pay just about anything for.it, and devote 	all
your 	time 	and 	energy to selling and doing that. Most people who fail to achieve.their
aspirations 	in 	business do the exact opposite. They let themselves be consumed doing
things of 	low 	value, 	then 	are surprised 	to 	discover they reap small harvests. To be fair,
getting yourself organized and disciplined to be relentlessly focused on creating value by
making yourself more 	valuable- 	daily- 	by always learning more, acquiring and
processing more infortnation, running more experiments, making more useful contacts;
and 	by 	doing the most valuable things you can do is not an easy task. 	I've 	written four
different books that reveal different paths converging at this same point. Association with
others working on this same skill is helpful. Dis-association from those not dedicated to
this 	premise is also helpful. 	. •
Which brings me 	to 	the other sign. He had zero tolerance for adults still sucking their
thumbs. 	If you 	wanted to whine and cry like a baby when something didn't go your way,
you 	were told to 	go 	home, stick a pacifier in your mouth and hug your 	blanki~. 	At 	the
time 	I'm 	writing this, the economy's a bit tough 	anc;l 	bruising and I hear too many adults
who 	sound like babies. Actually, it's just one 	of those times that the entire economy woke
up 	on 	the wrong side 	of 	the bed grumpy and is telling everybody: thumb-suckers not
we~come 	here. And this must be your personal policy, 	if 	you are to prosper and thrive
while 	most others 	do 	not. Which is, incidentally, all the time; fewer than 5% have 95% 	of
th~ _wealth 	flow to them all the 	tim~; 	the disparity is just more stark, the judgynent 	of 	the
cntical marketplace more harsh and quick and visible at some times than others. You
need zero tolerance for thumb-sucking, personally, 	or 	by anybody you pettnit in your
~orld: 	staff, associates, vendors, even customers, friends, neighbors, media voices you
~1ste~ 	t?, 	authors 	you 	read. The power 	of association is an enormously powerful,
u~·eststtble 	force, for good or evil, for gain or loss, for growth or regression. You dare not
discount or underestimate it. You can and should strategically use it for advantage.
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 60 --

Down 	on 	The 	Farm
One 	?f _my 	earliest consulting clients was in agriculture; a company that sold highly
sophtsttcated soil analysis services to farmers, then taking dirt samples from all over the
farm and returning with a color-coded map revealing the different mineral deficiencies
and needs for 	diff~rent 	fertilization to optimize yield. The service paid for itself many
times over in increased yield. 	One 	of 	my 	contributions was identifying all the 'reasons'
the 	fann~rs 	had for not buying and crafting effective answers. There were many. Some
thought 	tt 	too 'fancy pants'. 	Some 	scoffed at the science. 	Some 	balked at price. But most
confessed that they 	weren't 	even doing all they already knew to do, to improve
productivity, so 	why 	invest in this? That response is right out 	of an old 	joke 	about the
Farm Bureau representative traveling farm to farm, urging the farmers to attend the
upcoming Bureau-sponsored seminar. 	"You'll 	learn all sorts 	of 	new 	infoimation 	and
ideas for better farming," enthusiastically gushed the 	rep- 	to which one wizened old
farmer growled, 	"Ain't 	usin' 	half 	of 	what I know now."
A lot 	of 	not-very-bright people equate intellectual input to eating food. 	If 	the
refrigerator's full, they 	don't 	need more. 	If they 	can't 	clean their plate, they most
assuredly 	don't 	want a second helping. 	That's 	sensible about food. You 	don't 	really want
your belly 	in 	endless·expansion. 	But 	the mind? You want all the stimulation 	it 	can get,
and 	it 	is 	fully capable 	of expanding to meet it, to sift and sort and organize as much as
you 	can 	put 	on the table 	in 	front 	of it. Furthertnore, you 	don't 	need to fully digest and
use everything you read, listen to 	or 	watch 	in 	order to get ample value from taking 	it 	all
in. 	The 	value 	isn't 	in 	quantity consumed. 	It's 	in gems unearthed. Personally, I process
infortnation 	by 	the pound and 	am 	happy to find a few little things I can use 	pro~tably.
One 	such goodie in a year's time justifies 	my 	reading a newsletter every month. And
value does 	not 	even require revelations 	of 	brand new things - 	if 	the input reminds you 	of
knowledge already 	in 	your possession, nudges you into acting on some slow-simmering
idea 	or 	intention, pushes you 	past 	procrastination on 	just 	one useful action, counters
negative and gloom 	'n 	doom 	media 	blather, 	it 	earns its keep. 	If 	you feel the need to
excuse yourself for 	not 	acquiring, investing 	in 	and processing infonnation, 	look 	for a
smarter 	one 	than (a) I 	can't 	use 	it 	ALL, 	or 	(b) 	I'm 	not using everything I already know.
That's 	dumb-farmer-thinking. 	It 	is 	NOT 	the thinking 	of 	exceptionally successful people.
Donald 	Trump 	says 	he 	gets 	up 	every 	morning 	at 	5:30- to read. 	Several daily
newspapers, professional newsletters, books. I imagine the overwhelming majority 	of
what 	he 	finds 	he 	already knows 	or 	has little interest in. 	I'm 	confident 	he's 	smart enough
not 	to 	care about that, 	but 	to 	be 	steadfastly hunting for the rare find 	of something 	he 	did
not 	know 	or 	a fresh, different perspective that triggers profitable thoughts. 	I'm 	fortunate
to 	know 	a 	lot 	of 	very rich entrepreneurs. I 	can't 	think 	of any who 	don't 	have piles 	of
books 	they're 	always behind 	on 	while buying more, who 	aren't 	in constant pursuit 	of
more 	infom1ation and ideas 	and 	inspiration.
The 	playwright Archibald Macleish observed that the only difference between a man and
a 	pig 	is 	his 	mind. 	Both 	man and 	pig 	must 	feed their bellies - and do, 	eften 	with
disturbingly similar gusto. 	Only 	some 	men 	feed their minds as regularly, constantly,
continuously 	and 	enthusiastically.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 60 --

•
RESOURUS 	AND 	RESOURUFIJlNESS 	.
.
Many 	people 	are stopped before they 	start 	or 	later 	stymied 	because 	of
"lack 	of 	resources." 	· In 	2008, 	for 	example, 	easy 	access 	to 	both 	commercial
and consumer credit dried up, and many 	small 	business owners found 	it
harder to 	sell 	their goods and services, 	especially 	those 	typically 	bought
on payments; many entrepreneurs 	claimed 	inability 	to 	start, grow, expand
or 	even fund the 	day 	to 	day 	operations 	of 	their 	businesses. However, great
companies were 	built 	from scratch absent 	credit 	availability 	in 	the 	1930's,
in the 	1970's, 	in the 	1990's, 	and the same 	thing 	is 	happening right now.
That's because 	RESOURCES 	represents 	only 	half 	of 	the 	equation;
RESOURCEFULNESS 	the 	other 	h~lf. 	Resourceful 	QeOQie 	always 	have
capital 	in the 	for!p 	of 	creative thought and action.
Arguably, 	access 	to 	certain resources may be 	temporarily 	taken away 	or
denied you 	or 	made more 	difficult 	and 	costly 	for 	you 	by 	others 	or 	by
circumstances - either general 	marketplace 	conditions 	as 	have impact on
many 	no~, 	such as recession, 	or 	personal circumstances, 	for 	example,
starting over after a business 	failure 	or 	bankruptcy 	or 	relocating 	to 	an area
where you have 	no 	contacts. However, resourcefulness cannot be _	denied
or .taken away 	by 	others 	or 	by 	circumstances. 	It 	is 	a personal 	choice.
•
•
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

(You 	may 	need 	them 	now, 	more 	than 	ever.)
Up 	in 	the 	air. 	It's 	a 	bird 	it's 	a 	1 	• 	'
flown to fo 	'f 	d 	' 	Pane- 	no, 	Its 	SuperDan. 	By 	the 	time 	you 	read this, I 	will 	have
. 	ur 	ct 	tes. 	an 	presented my 'no-fail follow-up seminar' 	and 	autographed books for four
~oups 	tn 	L.A. 	Chtcao-o 	NYC 	and 	0 	1 	d 	A d b 	· 	· 	·	0 • ' 	o 	' 	. 	r 	an 	o. 	n 	oy, are 	my 	arms 	tired. 	At age 	20, 	this 	m1ght 	have
been fun. It 	still 	beats worktng 	.&or 	a 	11'v1"no- 	· 	· 	b B 	· 	· 	· 	· 	· 	· 	·• 	• 11 o 	tn 	a JO 	. 	ut 	It 	ts 	annoytno- 	every tune I hear pohttctans
particularly 	a certatn presidential candidate, demonizing 	me 	for my 	;uccess 	and 	suo-o-esting 	that 	th~
Grand Canyon 	o-ap 	b t 	h' h 	· 	oo
o 	e ween my 	tg 	Income and others' 	low 	incomes 	is 	evil 	and 	must 	be 	narrowed
~y 	g?ve~ment 	~heft. 	In 	a recent episode of Gene Simmons' show, his daughter ruefully said 	to 	him,
I thtnk.I m taking on your work ethic." 	To 	which he said, 	"Good." 	I'm afraid I'm 	NOT 	the kind of
~ool-Atd® 	~e 	no-work-crowd wants 	to 	drink. I drive them away 	from 	Glazer-Kennedy 	with 	this
kind 	of ~alk. 	I m not. sorry. And now that 	an 	overly generous economy has turned irritable 	and 	more
de~~ndtng, 	and businesspeople and salespeople are having 	to 	dig in 	and 	work, 	I'm 	hearing a lot of
whtntng. Most 	s~lespeople 	haven't worked in ages. I was comparing notes about this with a friend
last week and netther 	of us 	could even remember the last time 	we 	were 	"prospected" 	by a
sal~spe~son, 	folio we? 	up on by a salesperson we've previously bought something 	from. 	There's
crytng 	In 	the car bustness, but no salesman or dealer from whom I've purchased 	new 	cars 	in 	the past
5 years has contacted me. There's mass exodus of real estate aaents in surrender but none I've0 	'
purchased a property from has contacted 	me 	to sell me another. Retail: from the clothing store in my
hometown where I spent 	$1800.00 	in my first visit two years 	ago ... from the clothing store in my
other home city, where I spent 	$500.00 	on first visit ... from a store in Vegas where I spent 	$2,000.00
on cowboy boots , no salesman has attempted contact. May they all be fired. May they all starve.
Yes, 	in 	my 	WEALTH 	ATTRACTION book, I write in-depth 	about 	liberating yourself from
the 	Work-Money 	Link. 	But 	I never said: 	don't 	work, 	period. 	Nor 	did 	I 	suggest resenting 	it. 	I
talked about injecting leverage into the equation, 	as 	many 	ways 	as 	possible: better customer or client
selection; premium prices or fees with higher profit margins; better systems, delegation, outsourcing;
smart use 	of media in place 	of manual labor; smart time management; money wisely invested. But I
never suggested any or all 	of it 	as 	a substitute for work. I presented it 	as 	an 	enhancement of work. 	It
would be more accurate, I suppose, 	to 	talk about breaking the strict hours for dollars link.
Leverage. 	So 	I want to promote my books. To 	do 	my four events in four cities in four consecutive
days I used a chartered jet costing over 	$45,000.00. 	Sure, I could have flown commercial for a
fraction 	of that cost; 	if 	careful and not choosy about routes and time, for 	as 	little 	as 	$3,000.00. 	But
not done in a four day window. Done this way to minimize consumption 	of one 	of my most valuable
assets, my time. Besides, I got InfusionSoft to pay for 	it. 	When self-made billionaire 	W. 	Clement
Stone talked about 	"OPM"- 	Other Peoples' 	Money- 	I listened very carefully. 	Plus, 	I tapped into
InfusionSoft' s lists, and laid the burden 	of 	logistics on them. But this also allowed me 	to 	tap 	into
Glazer-Kennedy lists 	and 	media in a different way, providing Bill with a 	way 	to 	serve Members,
promote to non-Members, and support our Independent Business Advisors in four major markets at
zero out-of-pocket cost. I created "banked 	leverage" 	with current or prospective publisher(s) by
doina 	promotion few other authors could or would do. I 	hear 	you: 	''but 	I'm 	not 	an 	author 	and 	not
inter~sted 	in promoting books, so this doesn't apply to 	~e." 	You wish.I 	~ould 	talk 	~b?ut 	YOUR
business 	.... 	be 	it 	selling 	insu1·ance 	or 	installing 	drapenes 	or 	blacksmtthing. 	But thts 	ts 	the way 	of
all Reneaade Millionaires: they're masters at creating time efficient promotion for themselves and
their businesses, with someone else picking up the tab. 	OPM, 	OPR, 	and 	OPC: 	_other 	P~?ples'
money, other peoples' resources and other peoples' customers. 	_But 	make no 	mtstak~: 	tt 	s not about
avoiding work. In those four days, I will 	do 	four days 	of hard, high pressure, exhausting 	work. 	But
I'm 	leveraging it every way I can.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 60 --

•
'
Into every garden, rain 	must 	fall. Today 	was a 	rainy 	day.
I tripped 	and 	fell 	down 	the steps 	in 	my garage taking the 	~ash 	out 	an?, 	several 	~ours
later, 	my 	right ankle 	is 	swollen 	up 	to small canataloupe stze and won 	~be~ 	wetght, 	and 	I
am 	attempting 	to 	work with Ziploc bags 	of ice cubes wrapped around 	tt 	With 	duct 	tape.
All 	day 	long, it has been what 	I 	call 'Moron Day'. 	On 	Moron Day, 	everybody, 	even
usually competent people, act like morons.
One 	of the 	many 	ways 	people routinely act like morons - 	today 	no exception - 	is 	in stark
contrast to Renegade Millionaire Entrepreneur behavior, so 	it's 	worth mentioning (again).
A 	key 	characteristic 	of those 	of 	us 	in 	the moneymaking 	big 	leagues is
RESOURCEFULNESS. 	Most people have zero resourcefulness. For example, today,
on Moron 	Day, 	I get a fax forwarded 	to 	me from Vicky from a professional provider of a
certain service 	who 	I have connected with a client. The service provider, today 	an
Unresourceful 	Moron, says he lacks the client's phone number and cannot call the client
until we provide it. Never mind that a quick check 	of 	my fat correspondence file on this
produces a memo dated 	October 	15\ 	some 	40 	days ago, to the service provider in which I
gave him 	all 	the client's contact info. Let's assume he really didn't have it. He could
lazily and pitifully 	do 	what he did: send a fax to me asking me for it and let days go by
without closing a lucrative deal AND pissing off the client AND pissing 	me 	off. 	Or, 	gee,
since the client 	is 	a big, giant brand-name company he could have (a) gone 	to 	its web site
and gotten the phone number (or e-mail address) there 	OR 	(b) called its store in his city, a
few 	miles from his office, listed in his White 	Pages 	and asked them for the home office
number or 	(c) 	called AT&T's national infonnation and gotten it from them. That would
be resourceful. And the reason this guy makes minor money instead 	ofthe 	major money
he should make given his nearly unique expertise, contacts and value is because he
routinely exhibits this sort 	of dysfunctional behavior. We have to babysit him to use him,
so 	we 	do 	so only when absolutely essential, and refer only 	as 	last option. I could easily
have put a 	few 	hundred thousand dollars in his pocket last year but did not, 	because·he's
too irritating to work with.
By the way, tripping, falling and spraining my ankle while taking out my own trash
certainly sounds rather mundane, doesn't it? I suppose some people expect me to have
servants in striped pants and waistcoats doing such things for me, but I do not. Thus the
mundane annoyances 	of life affect me just 	as 	they do most people. Like going to the
bank, which I also did today, since my "errand runner'' that I do employ for such things is
out of town all week. I hate going to the bank, and do so personally only a handful 	of
times each year. Today, I had nine deposits, and some checks to cash. There was no one
else in the bank - because I timed it 	right 	So 	I had all three tellers. 	So _	you'd 	think I could
get out 	of there quick. Unfortunately, banks these days are run by barely-old-enough-to-
have-s~x-with-their-teachers, 	gum-popping, ipod-listening-to, acne-speckled girls 	to 	·
whom 	anythi~g 	bigger than 	$100.00 	is 	an 	unimaginable, frightening, dizzying sum; who
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 60 --

cban't 	add_or 	cou~t; 	who 	can't communicate; and attempt continuina their conversations
a out thetr boyfriend 	h.l 	· 	· 	; 0
. 	. 	s w 	t 	e 	watttng 	on 	customers. Thus the simple act of making a 	few
deposit~ 	and cashtng a check became excruciatingly painful 	and 	turned into a nasty little
altercation. 	· •
.
Today was a "communication 	day", 	so I spent a couple hours responding 	to 	faxes, mail,
phone 	~essag~s 	chock full 	of Moron Day stuff. Somebody offering 	me 	the 	"privilege" 	of
promoting 	therr 	book 	to 	our lists - and requiring 	my 	response within 3 days. Questions
asked 	I'_v_e 	previous! y answered. A goofy past client had even surfaced, confused about
co_pywntmg 	drafts.that had been delivered 	to 	him 	in 	October 	of 	2004, 	two 	years 	ago. 	We
mtght as well be trying to talk about Amelia Earhart's flight plan. But I diligently had
Vicky set up a phone appointment for him, dredged 	up 	his files, refreshed 	my 	memory -
and then he missed his phone appointment. He will be offered a replacement in February.
Moron Day is pretty niuch irredeemable. My experience is, once it gets headed in this
direction, there's no turning it around. My usual response 	is 	to 	throw 	in the towel after a
few hours and 	go 	to 	the track or the zoo or take a 	nap. 	Today, however, 	my 	bulging,
pounding, ice-melting ankle ruled such diversions 	out. 	Since 	I 	was 	going 	to 	be in pain, I
figured I might as well pile it on, so I just slugged it out right 	to 	7:00PM. 	On 	the positive
side, I turned down two speaking engagements and three copywriting jobs, but also made
a very nice amount 	of money saying yes 	to 	one thing.
I'm 	sure you experience Moron Days 	too- 	hopefully not 	as 	a Moron, but on the
receiving end. Maybe you think you're the only one. You are 	not. 	Everybody attempting
to do a lot at a fast pace has such days occur from time to time. A 	lot of people make
theanselves 	unhappy 	by 	believing 	that 	other 	people zip along 	and 	never 	hit 	speed
buntps. 	It was a big disappointment when I got 	my 	first shiny, expensive 	new 	Li~c?ln
Town Car and birds shit 	on 	it. At the time, I thought there ought 	to 	be a law restncttng
birds to shitting only on old, bad junker cars. Actually, I still 	think 	that, but I've realized
no such sensible arrangement 	of things exist or will exist. Anyway, maybe you 	c~n 	take
some consolation the next time you encounter Moron Day or a twisted ankle or btrds
shining on your Lexus, that 	it 	all happens to the best 	of us. Even 	Oprah 	got snubbed 	and
didn't get a wedding invitation from Tomkat.
Fortunately, 	I've 	rarely seen two Moron Days back 	to 	back. 	So, 	tomorrow, 	I'm 	limping
over to my desk early and conquering something important.
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 60 --

•
.
No 	money ever 	really 	disappears. Even the 	35-billion 	exaggerated 	by 	media
reporting 	to 	more than 	So-billion 	"lost" 	to 	investors in 	the 	Bernie Madoff
scam 	or 	the hundreds 	of 	billions 	of 	dollars 	"lost" 	by 	auto-makers and
banks in the 	late-2008 	crash 	isn't 	actually 	lost. 	By 	those 	who 	had it, 	it 	is.
But 	it 	passed 	to 	others' hands and 	is 	still 	in 	circulation. 	And 	one 	of 	the
most important things 	to 	know 	about money 	is 	that 	it 	IS 	always 	moving.
Somebody is 	always 	buying, someone 	selling .
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 60 --

· 	· . 	ave 	~sino 	gamtng 	to 	one 	extent 	or 	another, 	U.S. 	casino 	companies 	have 	discovered
even 	more 	fertile 	ground 	abroad 	( 	· 	. 	. 	.ffl 	g1ven 	greater 	population 	density 	and 	explosively 	expanding
~ 	uence), 	and, 	of course, 	the 	internet 	has 	put 	access 	to 	gaming 	at 	everybody's 	desktop 	The
mdustry 	has 	also 	discovered 	w~~t 	the~ 	once 	believed 	had 	to 	be 	given 	away 	free 	to 	indu~e 	people
to 	ga~ble 	can 	be 	sold 	at 	ever-ns1ng 	pnces, 	making 	lodging, 	dining, 	entertainment 	and 	shopping
the 	btgger 	pro~t 	~reducer. 	You 	~an 	sti~l 	find 	a $99 	room 	and 	$9.95 	buffet 	in 	Vegas, 	but 	it's 	certainly
not what 	~e 	CI!Y 	IS 	about. 	Cons1der th1s 	a metaphor.for your 	opportunities 	as 	well 	as 	fpr 	this
~ewsletter ~ 	pnmary 	puro.ose: 	erasing 	boundaries 	that 	long 	ago 	ceased 	existing 	in 	reality, 	that
~Inger 	only 	1n 	your 	own 	m1nd. 	Geographic 	boundaries 	are 	unimportant. 	Affluent 	people 	will 	fly
cross-country 	to 	d~ctor 	or 	financi~l 	advi~or 	or 	pet 	trainer 	or clothier ... .import from 	across 	country
or 	globe, 	steaks, 	w.1ne, 	candy, 	pat1o 	fum1ture. 	The 	internet 	has 	facilitated 	such 	expansion 	of 	local 	to
~lobal, 	and 	~marketing 	from 	global 	to 	local, 	as 	never 	before. 	I am 	not 	at 	all 	unique 	that 	my 	#1
Investment manager 	is 	in 	Boise, 	Idaho; 	the 	bulk 	of 	my 	real 	estate 	investments 	near 	Gedar 	Rapids,
Iowa; 	my 	custom 	shirts 	ordered 	from 	a store 	in 	London, 	England; 	my 	dentist 	in 	Richmond, 	Virginia;
and 	my 	most 	recent 	jewelry 	purchase 	not from 	my 	local 	store, 	but 	from 	DeBeers 	in 	New 	York 	(by
phone) 	although 	I 	live 	in 	none 	of 	those 	places. 	Whether 	you 	are 	merchant 	or 	expert, 	the 	S-mile
driving 	radius 	'rule' 	is 	as 	antiquated 	as 	my 	Smith-Corona 	typewriter. 	Price 	boundaries 	are 	self·
inflicted. 	Proof 	is 	abundant, 	in 	every 	imaginable 	product, 	service 	and 	business 	category, 	with
marketers 	on 	Planet 	Dan, 	that 	price-elasticity 	has 	never 	been 	greater- 	when 	selling 	to 	those 	least
concerned 	with 	price.
Yes, 	there 	are 	current, 	temporary 	economic 	woes 	reaching 	even 	the 	affluent 	Yacht 	repossessions,
for 	example, 	have 	jumped 	to 	nearly 	double 	their 	average 	level- 	but 	then, 	sales 	have 	been 	at 	an
all-time 	high 	for 	the 	past 	handful 	of 	years. 	To 	great 	extent, 	natural, 	inevitable 	ebb 	and 	flow. 	But,
recession 	notwithstanding, 	we 	still 	have 	a greater 	number 	of relatively 	new 	and 	ascending 	'middle
class 	millionaires' 	than 	ever 	before, 	as 	well 	as 	a large 	and 	pretty 	stable 	ultra-affluent 	population,
plus 	convergence 	with 	boomers 	holding 	a greater 	concentration 	of 	discretionary 	spending 	power
than 	any 	previous 	50 	to 	70 	year 	old 	group. 	I 	urge 	reading 	the 	Chapter 	titled 	NO 	BOUNDARIES 	and
contemplating 	the 	many 	examples 	of this 	idea 	in 	my 	new 	NO 	B.S. 	MARKETING 	TO 	THE
AFFLUENT book. 	And, 	in 	next 	month's 	NO 	B.S. 	MARKETING 	TO 	THE 	AFFLUENT 	letter, 	I'll 	be
bringing 	you 	an 	interview 	with 	Mary 	Brown, 	co-author 	of the 	book 	BOOM: 	Marketing 	To 	The
Ultimate 	Power 	Consumer- 	The 	Boomer 	Woman. 	If this 	interview 	doesn't 	motivate 	you 	to 	go 	after
this 	market, 	you 	can't 	be 	motivated. 	(Check 	and 	be 	sure 	you 	can 	still 	fog 	a mirror.)
Boundaries 	are 	no 	longer 	enforced 	except 	by 	marketers' 	apathy 	or 	resistance 	to 	change.
The 	concept 	of all 	limitations 	being 	internal 	has 	never 	bee 	truer. 	The 	surest 	path 	to 	prosperity
clearly 	and 	inarguably 	leads 	to 	and 	through 	service 	engineered 	and 	designed 	for 	and 	delivered 	to
affluent 	clientele, 	yet 	most 	business 	owners 	fail 	to 	"re-invent" ·inside 	out •. bo~m 	t? 	i?P: 	for 	this
opportunity. 	Their 	reasons 	are 	rarely 	practical. 	Oft~n, 	they 	have 	to 	do~~~ 	bemg 	1ntim1dated 	by 	the
wealthy· 	feeling 	out 	of their 	league. 	There 	is 	an 	entire 	chapter 	about 	th1s 	m my 	new 	book 	as 	well.
And 	yo~ 	might 	also 	read 	or 	re-read 	my 	book 	co-authored 	with 	Dr. 	Maltz, 	The 	New 	Ps~cho

with 	this 	application 	in 	mind: 	your 	own 	attdudes 	about marketing 	to 	the 	affluent

already 	found 	the 	prospects 	you 	want. 	Here 	is 	a hst 	of 	the 	publications 	1n 	~h1ch 	NetJets

You 	might 	notice ..... some 	are 	travel 	magazines, 	but 	many 	are 	not. 	There 	are 	the 	obvious
. upscale 	city 	magazines, 	from 	urban 	capitols 	with 	high 	density 	affluent 	populations 	- 	Boston,
Washington 	DC, 	Philadelphia, 	and 	destinations 	frequented 	by 	the 	affluent- 	Aspen, 	Las 	Vegas.
There 	are 	less 	obvious 	ones, 	too, 	such 	as 	art 	collector, 	thoroughbred 	racehorse 	owner 	and
yacht 	owner 	magazines. 	And 	a very 	fertile 	category 	most 	never 	think 	of: 	top 	university 	alumni
magazines. 	Many 	of these 	magazines 	also 	have 	their 	subscriber 	lists 	on 	the 	rental 	market, 	thus
making 	their 	readers 	accessible 	to 	you 	on 	local 	or 	regional 	basis 	rather 	than 	only 	national.

-- 14 of 60 --

ome
IS 	ANYBODY 	STILL 	JUST 	"THROWING 	AWAY 	MONEY" 	IN 	THIS
ECONOMY? 	Somewhere between the crash and hasty govrnment rescue of
Bear-Stearns and the jobs report showing 	80,000 	jobs evaporated in one month,
a 	38 	year old trader and real estate investor rounded up a group of his buddies
and 	took 	them 	on 	a 	"buddy 	trip" 	by private helicopter and Hummer limos; to 	VIP
lodging in South Beach; and a game of 'extreme adventure paintball' organized
by 	game-entrepreneurs, 	all 	former DEA agents. 	He isn't as weird as he sounds.
In 	fact, 	sellers 	to the rich and super-rich report a 	"profound 	EAGERNESS to keep
spending" 	on 	the part of those hit with investment losses they wish to view as
temporary, as a reinforcement 	of 	their personal belief system and 	status- 	doing
so emphasizes their sense of superiority over the masses. 	The 	NY 	Times
reported 	on 	spending for lavish holiday parties in and around 	NYC
unabated .... on a high-end real estate buying boom fueled by discounting: in 	07,
fewer 	than 	20 	Manhattan condos priced above 	$1O-m ill ion sold, in 	08, 	over 	70 	in
that 	price range ..... and a number of record prices achieved at art auctions this
season. 	And 	one 	person's thoughts about money being thrown away are
another's 	idea 	of 	justice. 	In 	discussing a 	professionally 	planned 	$200,000.00
wedding, a 	41 	year old woman marrying a guy she had dated in high school,
noted that she was a breast cancer survivor. 	The travel and entertainment
concierge service in Manhattan that arranged the helicopter to South Beach and
paintball 	adventure, 	In 	The Know Experiences, reports 	str~ng 	s~le~ 	still 	o~
everything from cigar parties to rentals of racing boats to 	flights 	rn 	ftghter 	Jets.
(Source: 	NY 	TIMES.com)
Reprinted 	from: 	NO 	B.S. 	MARKETING 	TO 	THE 	AFFLUENT 	LETIER
Subscribe 	@ Dan Kennedy .com
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 60 --

•
.
You 	know the one: 	if 	you can keep 	your 	head 	while 	all 	about
you, others are losing theirs .....
In 	uncertain, changing conditions we perceive as threats, 	it 	is
difficult 	to maintain any 	equilibrium, 	and 	it 	is 	easy 	to 	be
. 	paralyzed. 	It's 	not as 	simple 	a matter as 	optmism 	vs.
pessimissm or the old positive thinking 	v. 	negative thinking. 	It's
about the 	quality 	of 	your decision-making, the 	leadership 	you
exhibit to others you influence (staff 	or 	clientele), 	and whether or
not you 	act 	creatively.
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 60 --

.
The 	Plain 	tmth 	about.panic:.
Why 	does so much panic s 	d 	· 	.
h . 	. 	prea 	so 	quickly 	and 	easily	· 	through so many people whenever
tou~ 	times 	occu 	· 	· 	do 	rIll 	a 	given 	Ill 	ustry 	or 	profession, 	or 	the national economy?
Without deep roots, the tree topples in the storm. What are 'deep roots' for the business
o~er, 	entrepreneur 	or 	sale~ 	professional? 	One, 	a system 	of selling and conducting
bu~mess 	that 	h~s 	prove~ 	rehable and profitable, from which he can draw confidence, and
whtch 	he 	can 	stick 	to 	~th 	reasoned persistence. 	If 	he is advertising and marketing to
attract 	customers~ 	selhng to 	~em, 	r~tailling 	and multiplying them absent system, by
random and 	erratic 	and even 	Impulsive 	acts., with unplanned and disorganized effort, he'll
be 	blown 	off course 	by 	a stiff breeze.
Two, 	a support network 	including continuous in-flow 	of legitimately useful how-to
infortnation, coaching 	by 	people themselves tested by tough times and proven successful,
encouragement and motivation from like-minded, optimistic, forward-thinking
businesspeople. Absent this, with no counter-balance to the relentlessly and excessively
negative voices 	of media and peers, 	he'll 	bend then break early not late .
.
Three, 	a productive 	and 	profitable personal philosophy 	sharpened with reading, listening;
study 	of successful achievers; association with the confident and capable, so that 	he 	is
conditioned to be opportunistic, 	to search for and exploit the opportunities always
concealed behind adversity. 	If he 	is 	not 	daily improving his personal philosophy, 	he 	is
weakening from the inside out, the core hollow. When Bilbo Baggins said, 	"I 	do not like
adventures because they make 	me 	late for dinner", 	he 	expressed profound personal
philosophy governing thoughts, actions and outcomes. When someone says something as
simple as 	"I 	don't 	have time to process all that 	infortnation- 	I have 	my 	business to run",
he 	unwittingly reveals profound personal philosophy as well. You have to investigate
your 	own 	personal philosophy and evaluate how helpful 	or 	unhelpful it is likely to be in
weathering storms, in navigating 	roug~ 	seas, 	in 	unearthing hidden treasure.
A productive and profitable, 	pers,onal 	success philosophy. A sound, organized system. .A
trustworthy support network. These assets do 	not 	develop 	by 	accident 	or 	luck 	or
randomness. Successful people very deliberately work at developing them, seek to
connect to 	the 	people and resources and associations most 	h~lpful 	in 	developing them 	-.
and once connected, never risk disconnecting. They strategtcally assemble these assets tn
keeping 	with 	their own goals, strengths and weaknesses. Key word: strategically.
-
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 60 --

•
Y 	11 	Can 't 	manaO'e 	time. (I lied with the book title.) 	You 	can 	manage 	people 	andou 	rea 	y 	o 	. 	. 	. 	. 	.
the 	rules 	by 	which 	they interact with you, thetr pertntsston and 	oppo~ntty 	to 	tn~errupt
you 	and 	disrupt your productivity; 	you 	ca_n 	manage 	tec~ology 	and Its 	de~and.t~g 0
intrusiveness. Not managing these 	two 	thtngs, the two chtef causes 	of chrontc, 	nstn0
stress 	and 	unhappiness over time escaping your grasp. 	And 	you 	can 	manage 	your 	own
expectations, 	and 	try 	your best 	to 	manage the expectations 	of the others 	~round 	~ou. 	But
you 	can't actually manage time. It refuses to submit to any manmade 	policy. 	It fltes ..
In 	a coaching call I 	was 	asked 	about 	the 	"how 	to" 	of 	the 	Renegade 	Millionaire
Principle 	of "simultaneous, 	not 	sequential," 	as 	if 	there 	were 	how-to 	steps, 	1-2-3-4, to
it. 	The 	entire point 	is, 	there aren't. There is 	Step 	I 	and only 	Step 	I: 	start a sbitload 	of
things 	all 	at 	the 	same time. That's the entire seminar on this subject. 	Send 	a whole
bucket of 	balls 	rolling down the mountain side 	all 	at once. Then chase them. 	Some 	will
bear left 	or 	right 	and 	fly off the side of the mountain 	nevetto 	be seen again. 	Some 	will hit
rocks 	and 	shatter into a million shards, 	D.O.A. 	There will be chaos. A mess. Blood
spatters 	all 	around. 	But emerging from 	all 	of that, much more Accomplishment than
would 	occur or evolve 	any 	other 	way. 	Again, 	if 	you prefer something less messy and
more 	orderly, 	get 	a job. 	Be 	Dilbert in a cubicle. 	Somebody 	will give you one chore at a
time 	to 	do, 	that can be done 	i~ 	sequential steps. Unless and until the whole bureaucratic,
slow-moving company goes bust sequentially 	-like 	a corporate behemoth client 	of 	mine
is 	doing. 	Like 	Ford is doing. 	· 	·
And 	where the devil 	did 	this 	''please, 	no 	stress" 	baloney 	come 	from, 	anyway?
They're de-stressing school: 	no 	dodge ball, 	no 	score kept in T-ball, in some schools 	no
grades. 	When 	Imus 	calls a bunch 	of COLLEGE 	ATHLETES 	a name, they whine and cry
and 	run 	to 	mommy, 	unable 	to 	withstand the 	horror 	and stress 	of 	the attack. 	(Sure, 	Imus
was 	an 	~s 	to 	say 	it. 	But 	~hat's 	what he is: a Professional Ass. 	Somebody 	should have told
them: 	suck 	and 	stones mtght break your brittle little bones but names aren't supposed to
hurt 	you. 	Now 	stop whining or I'll take off my belt and 	2ive 	you brats 	somethinu 	to
whine 	about.) 	Everybody's growing 	up 	thumb-suckers. Wen, 	if you are 	an 	entr:preneur,
you 	better 	be 	a stress camel, bubba, 'cuz you're gonna be awash in it. 	DailY. 	The more
you 	atte~pt; 	the 	more 	you 	achieve; the more 	you 	get; the more stress is attached. That's
the 	way 	tt s supposed 	to 	be. 	You aren't supposed to have a 	7-fi~e 	income three .homes,
a garage 	full 	.of toys, 	mill~ons 	in 	the 	bank, etc. without a 	millio; pounds 	of 	~tress. 	Maybe
the 	guy 	pus~mg 	a broom m the grocery store ought 	to 	get 	to 	live stress-free. But not 	yoU.
So 	get 	over tt 	and 	get 	to 	work. 	We need 	some 	people with 	thi 	k 	kin 	b 	d 	h 	ld· 	·11 1 · 	. 	c 	s 	, 	roa 	s ou 	ers,Iron 	wt 	compu 	stve drive and 	stronO' 	taV 	· 	·
' 	• 	e> 	men 	emottonaltmmune system to support our
fast-growtng over-population 	of wimps.

-- 18 of 60 --

•
"Every 	year 	back 	spring 	comes, 	with nasty little birds yapping their fool beads off
and 	the 	ground 	all 	mucked 	up 	with plants."
So 	said: (a) W.C. Fields 	or 	(b) Dorothy 	Parker 	or (c) Woody Allen ..
D?rothy 	Parker, 	an? 	if 	you 	h~ven't 	read 	her, 	you~ve 	missed one of the most vicious biting
Wtts 	and 	g~d 	cynics 	of 	all 	time. 	When you visit NYC, you can stay at or have 	a 	drink at
~e 	Algo~qutn 	Hotel, home for years of Dorothy 	Parker's 	famous roundtable, where
literary 	hons met to drink and spar.
''You 	can 	be 	married 	and 	bored 	or 	single 	and 	lonely. 	Ain't 	no happiness 	nowhere."
So 	said: (a) Chris Rock or (b) Elizabeth Taylor or (c) Ann Landers. The correct answer
is Chris Rock.
It just 	seems few people are really happy or even content with much. We are all too
eager to complain, myself included- 	and I stop myself often. Truth is, everybody does
have something to complain about because no business, 	no 	career~ 	no 	relationship, 	no
one's health, no life is ever free 	of problems, hassles, annoyances or disappointments for
very long. Having a lot 	of 	money helps but I doubt there's enough money, 	period~ 	to
insulate somebody from things worthy 	of 	complaint. I certainly have been willing 	to
spend any sum, have spent quite a bit, and brought in a dozen experts, technicians, people
from the 	manufacturer 	to fix my fireplace but, after 5 years trying, I still have a gas
fireplace that sometimes works and sometimes doesn't, with 	no 	rhyme or reason. It's not
as worthy 	of complaint as, say, coming home from Iraq missing a leg. But 	it's 	still
worthy 	of complaint. Right now, everybody's complaining incessantly about gas 	prices-
even though they pay more per gallon for bottled water and Starbucks, even though our
prices are a bargain vs. other countries, even though we could easily 	go 	out less and
cluster errands but don't, and even though the economy's booming. Nuts. 	W~ell, 	we're
never going to stop others or ourselves from complaining 	at 	times 	we 	should be
celebrating and giving thanks. To a degree, our ever-restless dissatisfactions and
complaints are the forces leading to invention, innovation and, in some cases,
improvement. But I would offer this observation, for whatever it's 	worth- 	the most
successful people I know keep more 	of 	their complaints to themselves than they air 	and
operate in a broad, general way, happy and enthusiastic, 	"on 	fire" 	about what they are
doing and where they are going.
I talk to a lot 	of people who complain about 	parts 	of their businesses, 	some 	of the work
they must do. Charlie 'Tremendous' Jones says: 	"If you can'.t get excited. about 	~e
miserable 	job 	you've got right now, 	you 'II never get a good JOb 	worth 	betng 	exctted
about.'' I 	think 	that's true hour by hour, day by day. Certainly there are lots and lots of
people who would follow you into your lucrative business if they could only do the
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 60 --

pleasant tasks - like kids licking 	the 	creme 	filling 	out 	and 	discarding the 	rest 	of the
cookie or cake. 	The 	reason 	there's so little competition 	at 	the 	top levels of the
prosperity pyramid in America is 	NOT 	barriers erected to keep riff-raff out and 	the
elite small in number; 	it's 	mostly because most people 	won't 	get 	their 	hands filthy
doing all the ugly tasks 	that 	are 	required in 	order 	to get to 	do 	the pleasant ones.
When I was speaking a lot, I 	got 	approached at least 	1,000 	times 	by 	people 	who wanted
to 	be 	on stage and speak to thousands and make 	$100,000.00 	in 	an 	hour 	or 	two. I found
none were eager 	to 	learn the craft, create and perfect a presentation; study 	the 	100 	or 	so
speakers and stand-up comedians I pointed them to; 	go 	find 	inconsequential venues like
local car dealership sales meetings and Chamber meetings 	to 	practice; 	to 	create their own
business filling seats so they could prove they 	could 	sell 	from 	the 	platfortn before asking
someone to give them a valuable slot; then develop marketing materials; relentlessly 	mail
to people who might hire them; write books and articles 	and 	newsletters 	to 	create
prominence. 	And 	it 	doesn't 	take long for most people 	to 	complain a 	lot 	about the endless
hours in airports, the delayed 	or 	missed flights, 	the 	bad 	hotels, 	the 	bad 	food. Everybody' d
love to 	be 	rich. 	Most 	people just 	aren't 	willing to 	put 	up 	with 	all 	the 	crap 	you 	have to
shovel and occasionally swallow for 	the 	privilege. 	In 	this American life, you 	pick 	your
place and the prices you will 	pay 	for 	admission- 	so 	you 	really 	have 	little right to
complain about either.
So 	if this is 	one 	of 	those days, think twice before complaining. Because 	the 	secret 	of
secrets that 	we 	know and never speak 	of is that 	our 	exceptional success and prosperity
has only a little to do with all 	the 	things those wishing 	they 	had .what 	we 	have think 	it
does- 	with education 	or 	expertise 	or 	who-you-know 	or 	luck, etc. 	What 	we 	know that we
won't 	speak 	of 	is 	it 	mostly has 	to 	do with a willingness 	to 	do a 	lot 	of 	things others can do
but won't. 	.
•
..
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 60 --

Following, 	are 	a couple 	of 	my 	weekly 	columns 	published 	online 	by 	BusinessAndMedia.org, 	the 	s.ite
of 	the 	famous 	Business 	And 	Media 	Institute, 	arm 	of 	the 	Media 	Research 	Center. 	BMI's 	purpose 	IS
to 	bring 	accuracy 	and 	perspective 	to 	media 	reporting 	on 	business 	and 	economic 	matters- 	where
accuracy 	is 	so 	often 	sadly 	lacking, 	and 	media 	bias 	and 	political 	agenda 	clouds 	facts 	as 	opinions,
and 	conveys 	false 	impression 	by 	deliberate 	omission. 	Since 	these 	are 	weekly 	columns, 	and 	I did
not 	rewrite 	them 	here 	as 	1did 	most 	of 	the 	other 	pieces, 	there 	are 	very 	timely 	and 	now 	out-dated
references, 	but 	the 	core 	content 	is 	still 	relevant. 	You 	can 	read 	my 	current 	columns 	posted 	almost
every 	Wednesday 	at 	BusinessAndMedia.org
In 	the 	classic, 	celebrated 	book 	on 	success 	methods, 	THINK 	AND 	GROW 	RICH, 	its 	author
enumerated 	seventeen 	principles 	of 	success 	he 	observed 	used 	In 	common 	by 	hundreds 	of the
greatest 	inventors, 	industrialists 	and 	entrepreneurs 	of 	the 	1920"s 	and 	1930's 	(the 	book 	researched
over 	20 	years, 	commencing 	in 	1917; 	first 	published 	in 	1937). 	One 	of 	the 	seventeen: 	accurate
thinking. 	The 	significance 	of this 	is, 	in 	part, 	that 	it 	went 	against 	the 	immense 	popularity 	at 	the 	time
of 	"positive" 	thinking 	and 	unbridled 	optimism, 	widely 	promoted 	by 	other 	authors 	and 	lecturers. 	Hill
argued 	for 	fact-based, 	rational 	analysis 	and 	thought. 	This 	portion 	of 	his 	material 	is 	largely
overlooked 	or 	minimized 	by 	those 	studying 	it or 	those 	creating 	subsequent 	works 	derivative 	of it,
but 	Hill 	never 	meant 	the 	seventeen 	principles 	as 	cafeteria; 	instead, 	as 	matrix.
Accurate 	perspective 	about 	all 	aspects 	of recession, 	recovery 	and 	an 	merging 	New 	Economy - 	as
well 	as 	about 	what 	actually 	produces 	success 	in 	America 	- 	is 	an 	important 	navigational 	device, 	to
avoid 	being 	distracted 	and 	discouraged. 	Mostly, 	in 	these 	weekly 	columns, 	1try 	to 	provide 	that
perspective. •
•
•
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

Wiii'The 	Secret 	Mil·	lionaire' 	Arrive 	At 	Your
Door?
Not 	if you're 	sitting 	around waiting 	for 	him .

By 	Dan 	Kennedy
Business 	& Media Institute
1211012008 	2:32:30 	PM
.
A 	new 	TV 	program. 	loosely 	swiped 	from 	a 	TV 	show 	of
tong 	ago, debuts tonight 	on 	Fox. 	As 	I 	understand 	it: 	in 	"The
Secret 	Millionaire~ 	we 	wm 	see 	miUionaires 	go 	undercover,
vvork 	for a 	week 	in 	some 	poorly ·paid job 	and 	Uve 	as 	the
oppressed 	workers and 	economically 	troubled 	five. 	The
milUonaires 	will 	find one 	of ,the 	downtrodden 	they meet
especially 	worthy 	and. 	revealing 	themselves 	as 	"secret
millionaires,• 	will 	whip 	out 	their 	checkbooks 	and 	give 	the
deserving 	individual 	big 	bundles 	of money. 	lfs 	sort 	of a
privatized bailout
:. 	.t.
.:.·.J 	~f): c::m

Jflt 	be 	exciting: 	like 	Oprah 	gifting 	cars. 	People 	will 	cry, 	just 	like 	on 	the 	home 	make-
over show, where 	they 	give 	people 	remodeUng 	jobs or 	entire 	homes 	for 	free. 	On 	the
surface, 	there's 	nothing 	se~ousfy 	wrong 	with 	it, 	and 	its 	ciever 	television, 	especially 	now. 	It
does, 	as 	I 	said, 	borrow 	from 	a TV 	show 	of decades 	back: 	when 	a 	millionaire 	a11 ived 	as a
surprise 	on 	a 	doo1 step.
"Secret Millionaire· 	even 	borrows a 	bit 	from the ancient ..Queen 	For 	a 	Day" 	show -
because 	thafs 	aU 	it 	is: 	like 	tottery 	winnings~ 	n1ost 	entirely unearned 	windfalls 	disappear '"in
a 	dayl1f 	leaving 	behind 	more harm done 	than 	good 	"Give 	a 	man 	a fish, 	feed 	him 	for a
day. 	Teach 	a 	man 	to 	fishn- 	and 	require 	that 	he go 	fish- 	"feed 	him 	for 	life."
.
Therein 	is the 	problem. 	In 	•Secret 	Miifionaire:f 	we 	can 	see TV 	reflecting 	and 	reinforcing
societal and 	pofrticaJ 	trends 	that 	put us 	all 	in 	periL
•
This 	is 	basically 	a 	ga1 ne 	show 	with 	no game. Most game shows still 	require 	some 	skil~
and 	contestants 	actually 	prepare by 	drilling 	in 	advance 	for 	'Jeopardyf r 	or 	getti~ 	into fit
shape 	for 	'Survivor~ • or rehearsing for 	'American 	Idol.' 	The tireless 	1Mleel 	Of Fortune'
requires 	you 	know 	the 	cflfference 	between 	vowel 	and 	consonant 	On 	NBC's Wildly 	popular
"Deal 	or 	No 	DeaJ: they 	wisely 	eliminated 	aJl 	requirements 	of knowing 	anything 	or 	having
any 	skills; 	you 	need 	only 	guess 	the 	right suitcase and 	be 	able 	to 	tolerate 	Howie 	Mandell 	to
win a 	million 	dollars.
But 	"Secret 	MiUionaire» 	goes a 	step 	further. 	It 	asks 	nothing 	and 	hands out 	•noney for
free. 	It asks for 	no 	special 	preparation, 	effort, 	sJdiJ 	or talent 	in 	advance. 	And 	of course, 	it
asks 	for 	nothing 	in 	return.
-
/J.Js 	such, 	the 	show 	reinforces 	the 	dangerous 	idea 	so 	halrtrful 	to the 	working 	poor 	tiJat
simply 	because 	they 	are working 	poor, 	they 	deserve 	to have 	somebody 	hand thern a
bunch 	of money. 	They 	need 	only 	and 	hope 	for 	such 	an occurrence. 	Even 	better, 	the
money 	is 	1ransferred to 	them 	from 	the 	richr 	who 	have 	too 	much 	of it

WHJ "Th e S~>ret Millionair e' 	AJrlve At Your Door? 	•
..
Fox 	missed 	something 	here 	that 	would 	have 	made 	the 	show 	mu~ 	more 	popular. 	The
secret 	millionaires 	should 	be 	ripped from their mansions and office penthouses 	by 	force
and 	compelled .to 	play ~ 	their 	money 	iaken 	from them 	involuntari!y. 	But 	even 	as 	is, we
shouldn't 	miss 	the 	point 	that 	it 	could 	be 	called 	'The 	Re-Distribution 	Of 	Wealth 	Show.'
•
Nowl 	here's 	what 	you 	won't 	see 	on 	any major network: 	A 	show 	where 	rrhe 	Secret
~AHJio n aire " 	rewards 	people based 	on 	their 	productivity 	and 	investment 	of time 	and 	effort
Nobody 	doles 	out 	cash 	to 	those 	who 	have 	worked 	hard$educated 	themseives f scrimped
and 	saved1 	responsibly 	bought homes and 	cars 	they 	could 	afford, 	started 	and 	built
businesses, 	and 	created 	employment 	opportunities 	for 	others. •
Nor will 	you 	see 	a 	show 	called 	"The 	Self-Made 	Main 	Street 	Miltionairen 	about 	people
who 	have 	s+..arted 	with 	nothing 	or 	less 	than nothing and made fortunes 	through 	relatively
ordinary 	smaJI 	businesses 	and 	prudent financial 	behavior 	over 	time. 	There 	are 	far, 	far=far
more 	of those 	than 	there 	are 	Paris 	Hi1tons~ 	A-Rods and 	WaU 	Street 	types.
You 	won 't 	see 	th is 	not 	just 	because 	no 	one 	would 	watch, 	but 	because 	ifs 	reaiity TV
that would contradict 	the 	philosophical 	belief the media 	folks 	hold 	dear 	and 	the 	ideas 	1hey
wish 	to 	sell: 	Pulling 	oneself 	up 	by 	bootstraps 	is 	passe 	and 	freakish 	and 	no 	longer 	practical
ir. 	today's America. The 	Have's 	only 	have 	by 	luck 	and 	theft 	and 	have too 	much. 	And 	the
Have-Nots 	can 	onJy 	be 	helped 	by 	hand-outs.
Dan 	Kennedy 	is 	a 	serial 	ef!irepreneur, 	adviser to business owners, sought-after 	speaker
and author of 13 	books. 	More 	infonnation 	about 	Dan 	can 	be 	found 	at
www.NoBSBooks.rom, 	and 	a 	free 	collection 	of his 	business 	resources 	including
newsletters and 	webinar:s 	at 	vvww.DanKennedy. com .

Fiddlin' 	Around 	While 	Wall 	Street 	Burned
Serious 	economic 	issues exist, 	but 	media 	should 	show 	parts 	of
economy 	that 	still 	function.
By 	Dan 	Kennedy
Business 	& 	Media Institute
10/1612008 	1 :56:16 	PM

\Nhere 	were 	you 	when 	the 	lights 	went 	out 	on 	WaU 	Street?
•
•
i ... 	Prtmer-trtenctJy
._) 	. 	verslon
On 	Friday: 	Oct. 	10 , 	the first 	day 	the 	Doo 	dropped 	below 	8,000 	before 	crawling 	barely
above 	that 	at 	dose, a 	mend 	and 	I 	were in Atlantic 	City 	on 	·	a 	guys' 	vacation. 	We 	went to 	11
Mulino 	for dinner - the done 	of the famous New 	York 	City 	restaurant 	at 	Trump 	Taj
Mahal. 	We 	had 	imported 	cheeses 	and 	sausages 	and 	imported 	truffles 	shaved 	over two
types 	of 	pasta for 	appetizers. 	The 	truffles 	alone 	were $71. 	I 	had veal 	Osso 	Bucco 	with
risotto. 	He 	had a 	veat 	chop hand-pounded 	thin;·nghtly 	battered, 	with 	olive 	oil 	and 	-arugula
and 	tomatoes. The 	tab, 	with 	tip, 	was 	$540 .
.
We 	were 	not 	alone. 	I 	got 	the 	last 	7:00p.m. 	reservation. By 	7:30, 	every 	table 	was 	fulL
Most were 	orderi~g 	bottles 	of winel 	or 	behind 	us, 	glass 	after 	glass 	Of Taittinger, 	thus 	their
tabs even higher than ours. Few 	couples, 	most 	parties 	offourt 	eight 	12.
t 	remind 	you~ 	not 	in 	Beverly 	Hills 	or 	New 	York 	City 	or 	Londo~. 	In 	Atlantic 	City.
This 	is 	the 	untold ,story 	of the 	economy.
Between 	91 	and 	94 	percent of 	aU 	horne mortgages in the 	United 	States are· current,
not teetering 	at 	forectosure. 	The 	overwhelming 	number 	of banks, 	particuJarty 	community
banksl 	have 	been 	managed 	responsibly 	and 	are 	not ·on 	the 	brink 	of collapse. 	The 	vast
majority 	of 	public .	corporations 	with 	stock 	values 	depressed 	by 	20 	or 30 	percent are 	in 	no
way 	connected 	to 	the mortgage 	meltdown, 	Walt 	Streefs house 	of cards or 	the 	collapsing
auto 	industry. 	They 	are 	well-managed 	companies 	and 	their 	fundamentals 	are 	sound.. 	And
. 	there 	are 	still 	productivet 	suCCCSb1'ul, 	optimistic 	people.
Thus, 	on 	Friday, 	Atlantic 	City 	filled 	up. 	The 	show 	we went to 	had 	every 	single 	one of
its 	1,000 	tickets 	sold and 	seats 	filled 	so 	that 	a .$100 	tip 	~uld 	only 	II lOVe 	us from 	a 	bad
seat 	tc? 	an 	OK 	seat, 	not 	a 	great 	one. 	On 	return 	Saturday mid-day 	to 	Akron: 	Ohio, 	we
sb·uggted 	through 	dense 	baffic 	moving 	to 	and 	from 	stores and 	restaurants, 	tnostly 	made
up 	of ne-.v 	and 	nearty new 	automobiles, 	an 	populated 	by 	people 	talking 	on 	cell 	phones.
•
Why 	wasn't 	Atlantic 	City 	a 	ghost town? 	VVny werenlt 	the 	Akron 	streets 	as 	empty 	at
noon 	at 	midnight? 	V\fny 	weren't 	all 	the 	allegedly 	starving 	masses 	at 	home 	huddling?
,
Of course there are serious economic 	problems 	that 	require 	urgent fPcjng. 	That 	is 	best
done, ·largely, 	with 	private capital, not 	the 	stampede to 	sociaJism 	tnade 	palatable 	through

I 	say ,	: 	anybody 	can 	look 	good 	when everybody 	looks 	good. The 	pro
deserving admiration 	-and 	consistent 	exceptional 	reward and 	security- 	is
the 	person 	who 	fashions 	himself 	to 	do 	well 	when most others don't, 	won't
or 	can't. 	A 	lot 	of 	quarterbacks can 	throw 	the 	long 	ball 	with impressive
accuracy 	in 	the 	domed 	stadiums 	- 	so 	what? Let's see him throw 	it 	on a
snow 	and 	ice 	flecked 	field 	with 	20 	mile 	an 	hour 	winds. 	Every 	so 	often, an•
econom ~ic 	cycle 	separates men from boys, tough from weak, 	agile 	from
dull. 	In 	selling, 	salespeople 	often mimic 	poorly 	performing 	athletes 	when
they 	talk 	about 	"bei ,	ng 	in 	a 	slump." 	And 	slumps 	do 	occur, sometimes 	with
obvious 	stimulus, 	such 	as 	a recession; other times, 	solely 	as a 	result 	of
private reasons. Either way, 	it's 	not 	the getting into a 	slump 	or 	the
existence 	of 	slumps 	that 	matters. 	It's 	how 	you get 	yourself 	to 	"play 	your
way 	out."
•

www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 27 of 60 --
